questions.text: I believe that overthinking kills momentum and that action teaches faster than analysis.
Physical sensations accompany my emotions in a way that makes them impossible to ignore.
I believe my workspace should be arranged so that others can use it comfortably, not just me.
I am most productive when I have full autonomy over direction.
I do not naturally register when someone mirrors my body language to build rapport.
I prefer to serve on committees rather than chair them.
I feel that my home should always be open to anyone who needs a place to stay.
I find myself tracking the emotional trajectory of a conversation as it unfolds.
I would delay my own education to support a family member through theirs.
I believe that I should unapologetically pursue the things that excite me.
I keep a journal or log to reflect on my progress and hold myself accountable.
I think that a dispassionate, systematic approach to others' distress is the posture that most effectively prevents burnout and preserves long-term capacity to contribute.
I regularly step back so others have space to take initiative.
I feel that the motivation to respond to suffering is what gives my actions their moral substance.
I tend not to notice when someone is putting on a brave face.
I believe that personal success is a valid and worthy life aim.
I can tell when someone is interested in a conversation topic versus enduring it out of courtesy.
I enjoy being the one others look to for decisions.
I find that social hierarchies are mostly invisible to me unless someone explains them.
I take time to define the scope of a task before beginning work on it.
I tend to deal with maintenance issues only when something actually breaks.
I often feel like my emotional responses are running at double speed compared to the people around me.
I find that caffeine, sleep deprivation, or other stressors don't dramatically alter my emotional state.
I experience emotional pain and physical pain as remarkably similar — both sharp, both lingering.
I am comfortable telling people directly what I need from them.
I believe that maintaining analytical distance from emotionally charged situations is a skill that produces consistently better outcomes.
I pick up on when someone exhales audibly as a way of communicating displeasure without words.
I think that calm, reasoned assessment of painful situations is the approach that leads to the most consistently good outcomes.
I believe that when someone shares their suffering with me, the moral weight of their experience should shape what I do next.
I notice that my system treats emotional intensity the way a deep harbor treats waves — they enter but don't cause turbulence.
I believe that outcomes belong to the whole group, regardless of who takes command.
I get bored when a process becomes too routine and look for ways to shake things up.
I create and follow budgets for my personal and professional life.
I notice that surprises, interruptions, and disruptions produce only a small ripple in my internal state.
I tend to choose the fastest lane, the shortest line, and the most efficient option for myself.
I hold the view that the weight I give to suffering in my moral framework determines the ultimate shape and meaning of my life.
